figures.gloss: The primordial androgyne-mother who bore Mawu-Lisa and then withdrew utterly from the world's affairs, leaving creation to her twin offspring.
figures.facet: The deus otiosus stripped to her essence: a creator so absolute that she has no cult, no temple, no prayer — divinity defined precisely by its recession.
figures.provenance: Recorded chiefly through Herskovits' 1930s Dahomey ethnography; the creator's near-total silence in lived cult is itself the attested fact.

figures.gloss: The twin demiurge of moon-Mawu (night, coolness, mercy, the female west) and sun-Lisa (day, heat, strength, the male east), whose union orders and governs the made world.
figures.facet: A creator-pair held in a single hyphenated name: not two gods in tension but one balance spoken with two mouths — sky-parenthood rendered as deliberate, gendered complementarity.

figures.gloss: The moon-half of the creating pair: coolness, night, wisdom and gentleness, associated with the west and with the fertility of women.
figures.facet: The lunar reckoner cast as the elder, calmer wisdom — the moon as the seat of mercy rather than the mere measure of time.

figures.gloss: The sun-half of the creating pair: heat, day, strength and labour, associated with the east and the chameleon who carried his light.
figures.facet: A solar god who is one wing of a couple, never a lone sovereign eye — the sun bound by covenant to the moon.

figures.gloss: Youngest son of Mawu-Lisa and divine linguist: the guardian of the crossroads and every threshold, through whom alone the speech of gods and humans may pass.
figures.facet: The gate that must be opened first: no rite begins until Legba is fed — the trickster installed as the indispensable opening protocol of all worship.

figures.gloss: The system of destiny written in 256 signs, read through palm-nuts and casting-chains; the personal lot and the cosmic script each being holds and may consult.
figures.facet: Order not as a king's decree but as a readable text — fate rendered as a finite alphabet of signs, the cosmos made legible and queryable.
figures.provenance: Fa is the Fon reception of Yoruba Ifa; an attested borrowing between neighbouring peoples, the 256 odu shared in structure.

figures.gloss: Dan Ayido Hwedo, the rainbow-serpent who coils the world together and bears Mawu through the sky; with his consort Ayida-Wedo he is the arc of male and female, the motion that keeps creation from collapsing.
figures.facet: The cosmos held by a serpent's continuous motion, not slain by a hero: the snake as the world's living tendon and circulation, coiled beneath the earth in 3,500 turns above and below.
figures.provenance: Dan crosses the Atlantic as Damballa in Haitian Vodou; the rainbow-serpent complex is widespread across West Africa.

figures.gloss: The earth-god and lord of smallpox and pestilence — King of the Earth, feared and propitiated, whose name was so charged it was spoken only in circumlocution.
figures.facet: The earth itself as a dread sovereign of disease: fertility and death held in one ground, the great-mother who heals and afflicts with the same hand.

figures.gloss: The thunder-god who hurls stone axes from the sky, a son of Mawu-Lisa governing storm, rain and the justice of the lightning-stroke that strikes the guilty.
figures.facet: Thunder as moral verdict: the ram-voiced sky-axe that does not merely destroy but adjudicates, lightning aimed by righteousness.

figures.gloss: The vodun of iron, war and the forge — the divine smith and tool-bringer, Mawu-Lisa's instrument for clearing and shaping the world for human life.
figures.facet: Iron as the very means of civilisation: Gu is not only a war-god but the cutting edge by which forest becomes field, the smith fused with the world-clearing culture-hero.

figures.gloss: Spirits of the waters and of children born deformed or extraordinary — royal water-guardians and ancestral kings, deified anomalies who rule streams and pools.
figures.facet: The shard that sanctifies the anomaly: where many traditions cast out the strange-born, the Fon enthrone them as water-kings — divinity located in deviation itself.
figures.provenance: Closely tied to the Dahomean royal lineage; documented in Herskovits and in the cult of the kings' deified children.

figures.gloss: The leopard-sired founding ancestor of the Alladaxonu, the royal clan of Dahomey: legend holds him born of a union between a leopard and an Aja princess of Tado, his descendants migrating to found the kingship at Allada and later Abomey.
figures.facet: Dynastic legitimacy rooted in an animal sire rather than a sky-god's mandate — the kings of Dahomey ruled not by divine right handed down but as literal 'children of the leopard'.
figures.provenance: Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938); Edna Bay, 'Wives of the Leopard' (1998).

figures.gloss: The vodun heading the sea and its waters, grouped by Herskovits among the pantheons descended from Mawu-Lisa alongside earth and thunder; ruler of the ocean that bounded the kingdom's coast and carried its trade.
figures.facet: The sea rendered not as chaos to be crossed but as its own governed pantheon-seat, on par with earth and sky — the coast's danger and abundance both placed under one divine office.
figures.provenance: Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938).

figures.gloss: Son of Mawu-Lisa given dominion over the bush, its wild animals, and the hunters who enter it — one of the divided domains among the creator-pair's children.
figures.facet: The wilderness counterpart to Gu's cleared field: where Gu's iron makes forest into farm, Agé governs what the forest keeps for itself.
figures.provenance: Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938).

figures.gloss: The kpojito ('reign-mate') of King Tegbesu in 18th-century Abomey, a priestess whom tradition credits with bringing the cult of Mawu-Lisa to the capital and reorganizing state religion around the creator-pair.
figures.facet: The pantheon's political architect: the roster's central theology (Mawu-Lisa as installed at Abomey) rests on a named, datable human act of religious reform, not solely on unauthored myth.
figures.provenance: Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938); Edna Bay, 'Wives of the Leopard' (1998).

figures.gloss: The Fa diviner-priest who casts the palm nuts or the divining chain, reads the resulting du (sign), and prescribes the offerings or observances it requires.
figures.facet: The living interface without which the whole Fa system is inert text: destiny is legible only through his cast and his reading, every consequential household or state decision routed through his hands.
figures.provenance: Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938); cognate to the Yoruba babalawo of the neighbouring Ifa complex.

figures.gloss: The individual destiny-sign established for a person at initiation into Fa, kept thereafter as a sacred bundle and consulted through life as the object-form of one's own fate.
figures.facet: Fa's 256-sign cosmic order made personal and portable — not the readable text of destiny in general but the one page of it a given person actually owns and must serve.
figures.provenance: Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938).

figures.gloss: Small, elusive forest spirits of the Fon bush, said to have first taught hunters the use of gbo (charms) and the properties of leaves; approached with offerings at anthills and silk-cotton trees.
figures.facet: The origin of practical magic located not in a god's decree but in a benefactor-people of the forest — knowledge of gbo given, not invented, by beings older and smaller than humankind.
figures.provenance: Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938).

figures.gloss: The yearly cycle of ceremonies at Abomey feeding and honoring the royal ancestors, historically including the 'grand customs' held at a king's death and involving mass offerings and, per period accounts, human sacrifice.
figures.facet: The dead kings sustained as the state's true, ongoing government: legitimacy renewed not once at accession but annually, through continued material feeding of the royal ancestral line.
figures.provenance: Documented by 19th-century European visitors to Abomey (Dalzel, Forbes, Burton's 'A Mission to Gelele') and by Herskovits' ethnography.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Encoded, highly structural graphic symbols that translate the unseen world into actionable ritual reality.

The rainbow-snake as bringer of water, fertility and continuity — Australia's Rainbow Serpent and the Fon/Vodun rainbow-serpent Dan/Ayida-Wedo — both make the snake the cosmos's life-and-water principle, NOT a chaos-dragon to be slain. The cosmic-serpent shared across unrelated families.
GOLD SEAM: Cosmic serpents that bite their own tails to encircle the universe, preventing the forces of creation from scattering into nothingness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic serpent that surrounds reality and serves as the conduit for divine, structural energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The celestial rainbow functioning as a divine entity that bridges the heavens and the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cooling, balancing, and predominantly feminine/androgynous principle of creation that neutralizes volatile energy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The master of iron, fire, and occult energy who possesses the unique, highly dangerous capacity to shape human society.
GOLD SEAM: Parallel entities of the crossroads forged into structurally identical roles across New World diaspora traditions.
GOLD SEAM: Shares the exact attributes of iron, warfare, and technological mastery with the Dahomean Gu.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An objective, mechanical system for retrieving divine commands that removes the subjectivity of human ecstatic trance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A divine serpent intrinsically linked to rainbows and water systems, acting as a cosmic bridge.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An earth-wandering spirit of affliction and heat who possesses humans with disease, requiring strict ritual propitiation rather than simple worship.
Neighbouring West-African supreme makers who create and then withdraw from the world's affairs — the deus otiosus shared across the region, no proven common descent.
GOLD SEAM: A speculative structural parallel to the master of the crossroads and gatekeeper, though Baha' al-Din's ultimate act was to permanently lock the gate to the Divine.
GOLD SEAM: Cosmic serpents whose coiled bodies structurally support and bind the divine order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct regional cognate and shared theological complex across the neighboring Gbe-speaking peoples.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The identical crossroads trickster and linguist figure shared across Ewe and Fon traditions.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The shared geomantic divination system derived from the broader regional oracle complex.
The withdrawn primordial mother bears the active creating twins — recession above, governance below; one Fon cosmogony in two storeys.
Moon and sun as the two halves of one hyphenated creator within the same tradition — the complementary couple at the root of Fon cosmology.
Legba is the youngest son and appointed linguist of the creating pair — the gate they set between themselves and humankind.
The rainbow-serpent Dan bears Mawu through the heavens and undergirds the world the twins govern — serpent as the creation's load-bearing motion.
Fon Legba and Yoruba Esu/Elegba are near-identical crossroads-tricksters among neighbouring peoples — an attested shared cult, not a distant analogy.
Gu (Fon) and Ogun (Yoruba) are the same iron-and-war smith-god shared across the Fon–Yoruba neighbourhood — a documented regional cult.
Both reduce destiny to a casting-system over a fixed sign-set; offered cautiously, no contact — divination and board-cosmogram as two readings of fate.
Two traditions render the world as a balance of cool/dark/female and hot/bright/male principles — complementarity, not warring dualism.
Both name a single dual creator of paired male/female aspect at the head of the cosmos — divinity as a self-complementary couple.
The creating pair as the male and female poles whose union produces the lived world — structural rhyme with Purusa and Prakrti.
Sky-and-earth / sun-and-moon parenthood: world-parents whose union and roles order the made cosmos.
The supreme source who recedes from cult into impersonality — the withdrawn high-god with little direct worship.
Both luminary-deities head the bright order, but Fon assigns the calm sovereign light to the MOON and a female, where Shinto crowns the female SUN — the gendering of the luminaries reversed.
Solar power as heat, day and labour — but Lisa is one wing of a couple where Ra is the lone sovereign eye.
The serpent as cosmic sustainer and sky-bridge rather than slain dragon — feathered/rainbow snake as continuity, not chaos.
The world held together by a single coiling axis — serpent-as-world-frame beside tree-as-world-frame; a reach, flagged.
Neighbouring Fon and Yoruba thunder-gods who hurl stone axes and judge the guilty by lightning — shared West-African storm-cult, near-mirror roles.
Axe/hammer-wielding thunderer of the sky — the storm-wielder joint across unrelated families.
Ram-voiced thunder hurled from heaven; the lightning-striker as a recurring sky-figure.
The divine smith who forges the tools of war and civilisation — fire-craft as a god's office.
Divine artificer who shapes the implements of the world — the smith-joint across families.
The earth personified as great-mother — but Sakpata fuses fertile ground with the sovereign of pestilence, earth as dread as well as nurse.
The earth-mother who gives life and deals death in one body — terrible fertility shared across continents.
Divine messenger and god of the crossroads, translator between worlds and opener of the way — psychopomp-trickster across families.
An impersonal order the cosmos and each being obey — but Fa is a readable script of 256 signs where the Dao is the unspeakable way; a partial rhyme, flagged.
Both build a destiny-system from binary marks combined into a closed set of signs — odu and hexagram as cousins of method, no contact implied.
Within the tradition, the withdrawn grandmother-creator stands behind Mawu the moon — the recession that lets the active luminaries govern.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Retains the exact functional geometry of the Fon Legba, presiding over the crossroads as a morally neutral gatekeeper who must be appeased to access spiritual power.

GOLD SEAM: thunder as the executor of divine justice — Fon Xevioso strikes the wrongdoer with the bolt as Amadioha strikes the perjurer and murderer; the lightning read as a verdict across West African traditions. Both carry the ram as emblem.

GOLD SEAM — FLAG: Fon Legba and Igbo Ekwensu are West African trickster-powers of crossing, change, and negotiation, both demonized into 'devils' by Christian missionaries. The 'Legba=Devil' and 'Ekwensu=Satan' equations are imported dualism, not native theology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both centralize the serpent as a sacred, fundamental pillar of earthly vitality, refusing the narrative that demonizes it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both govern the morally neutral, deeply pragmatic manipulation of unseen intersections, prioritizing earthly results over high cosmic law.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial creator deity who establishes the cosmic order and then entirely withdraws, ceding direct governance to active, localized spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly specialized, tactile system of signs used exclusively by initiates to decode divine history and spiritual mandates.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The specialized divinatory practice that accesses divine will through a mathematically precise system of object manipulation, such as casting stones or nuts.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shared structural role where a specific liminal deity serves as the cosmic linguist and key-holder to the Otherworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ritual offerings made directly to a cosmic aquatic serpent dwelling in the deep waters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, cosmic mother figure who bridges the unapproachable, absolute creator and the active, generated universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The shared West African earth and pox deity, persisting across the Middle Passage into the Caribbean.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Using complex geometric ground-marks and sonic implements to articulate cosmic order and manipulate spiritual forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial female creator who birthed the cosmos and distributed its governance into dualistic spheres.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shares deep historical roots with the Fon master of languages and doorways, recontextualized as a monarch of sorcery.
Both are the oldest, most withdrawn source-being behind a working religion of nearer powers — the African high origin that makes the world and then steps beyond reach of cult.
Loose: both locate sacred potency in the body of a creature/power consumed and feared, but the link is faint and structural only. Flagged.
The rain-serpent face of !Khwa rhymes with the great cosmic snake of the waters and sky; both make the serpent the body of weather and abundance rather than a chaos-enemy to be slain.
GOLD SEAM: Twin branches of the same vast West African geomantic tradition, utilizing identical mathematical matrices to decode destiny.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize ultimate reality as an inherently androgynous, dual-natured entity containing all cosmic polarities.
GOLD SEAM: The sheer embodiment of martial violence venerated directly through iron implements without an anthropomorphic face.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A divine serpent associated with water courses, deeply intertwined with the legitimacy of the local pantheon and royalty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme, primordial androgynous creator who originates the cosmos and delegates active worldly rule to subordinate spiritual entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Consulting a divine or cosmic mechanism to divine the correct course of action for daily decisions.
GOLD SEAM: A sophisticated system of written cosmic signs that allows practitioners to decode the universe and intervene in destiny.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dual-natured divine parent encompassing both the solar/paternal and lunar/maternal aspects of creation within a single supreme entity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A physical divination system that maps the entire cosmology to diagnose moral and spiritual imbalances in the human sphere.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme white-clothed deity associated with the sun, creation, peace, and ultimate cosmic order.
GOLD SEAM: A direct historical and theological continuation of the Dahomean crossroad deity, softened into an elder in the Caribbean.
GOLD SEAM: Retains the exact Dahomean attributes of war, fire, and the path-clearing machete.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient python of Ouidah, preserving an unbroken, amniotic lineage back to the African source.
GOLD SEAM: The Dahomean transcendent creator mapped seamlessly onto the French Catholic God.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct descent of the Dahomeyan/Fon trickster and opener of the crossroads.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial creator conceptualized as an indivisible, dual-gendered couple.
Both are the oldest, most withdrawn source-being behind the working pantheon — the African high origin that creates and then steps back beyond reach.

dual-natured rainbow serpents who carry water between earth and sky and ensure cosmic continuity, from the shared Fon-Yoruba coastal West African religious complex feeding both Dahomean Vodun and Candomblé.
the elder, primordial water-and-mud deity of the Fon pantheon, carried into Candomblé's Jeje-Nagô synthesis under an almost identical name.
the same earth-and-smallpox god-complex shared by neighboring Fon and Yoruba coastal religion, fused in Candomblé's Jeje-Nagô houses much as Ogum absorbs the Fon Gu.
West African-descended divinatory systems for discerning the gods' will, using different technical media — cowrie shells versus Fa's divining chain.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the same Gbe-speaking thunder-cult under its Ewe and Fon names, paralleling the file's existing Mawu-Lisa and Legba/Fa cognate seams between these neighboring peoples.
The leopard-sired founder of the royal line is the first of the ancestors the Annual Customs exist to feed — the dynasty's origin and its ongoing ancestral maintenance are one continuous cult.
Tradition credits Hwanjile by name with installing the Mawu-Lisa cult at Abomey — a historical human act behind the roster's central theology.
The diviner and the divination-system he alone can read are functionally inseparable within the tradition.
The personal sign is issued from, and remains a portion of, the same 256-sign system.
Two sons of Mawu-Lisa dividing the world between them: Agé keeps the wild bush, Gu clears and forges the field — bush and field as one completed domain-split.
Agbe heads the sea pantheon as one of the creating pair's offspring-domains, alongside Legba's crossroads and Dan's coiling of the world.

West African sea-headed vodun and the Middle Passage's Iemanja both make a single sovereign of the ocean's danger and abundance — no direct attested descent from Agbe specifically, structural rhyme only.
Both are origins of the tradition's practical supernatural craft — Aziza gave the leaves and charms, Fa gives the readable signs — a loose within-system rhyme, not a proven single source.

both center a sacred, protected serpent that moves freely among the living as the deity's own messenger and may not be harmed — Igbo Idemili's royal python and Fon Dan's identity as a python-god of Whydah, across unrelated West African branches.
direct Dahomean (Fon) descent — the Fodu serpent complex carried into Suriname's Winti pantheon.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Fon people of the former Kingdom of Dahomey (now southern Benin) — the West African Vodun tradition from which Haitian Vodou and related New World religions descend. It venerates the vodun, a large body of gods and spirits headed by the dual creator Mawu-Lisa (offspring of the withdrawn primordial Nana Buluku), through cult-houses, initiation, spirit-possession, and the Fa divination system. It remains a living tradition with a wide Atlantic diaspora.
dossier.cosmos: Creation issues from the withdrawn androgyne Nana Buluku through the twin Mawu-Lisa — moon-and-sun, female-and-male — whose complementarity orders the world, while the rainbow-serpent Dan (Ayido Hwedo) girdles and moves the cosmos and keeps it from collapse. The pantheon is organized into families of vodun governing sky (the thunderer Xevioso), earth (Sakpata, lord of the ground and of smallpox), iron (Gu), and water. Each person holds a destiny (Fa) inscribed in one of 256 signs, and the ancestors and the deified anomalous dead (tohosu) remain powers among the living.
dossier.practice: Vodun is served in initiatory cult-houses where devotees are consecrated to a particular deity and may be possessed by it in drumming-and-dance ceremonies; worship centers on sacrifice, offering, and the mediation of priests (vodunon) and diviners (bokonon) who read Fa with palm-nuts or the divining chain. Legba, the trickster-linguist of the crossroads, must be propitiated first so that communication with the gods can open.
dossier.canon: Oral — cosmogonic myth, the divinatory verse-corpus of Fa (the Fon reception of Yoruba Ifa, sharing the 256 signs), praise-poetry, and cult-song — documented ethnographically, above all by Melville J. Herskovits.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds ordered multiplicity — a single withdrawn source unfolding into a governed pantheon of specialized powers and a legible 256-sign map of destiny, the sacred distributed as a working division of cosmic labor.
sources: Melville J. Herskovits, 'Dahomey: An Ancient West African Kingdom' (1938)
Suzanne Preston Blier, 'African Vodun: Art, Psychology, and Power' (1995)
